name: H-1B Visa
shortDescription: The H-1B visa allows U.S. employers to temporarily employ foreign workers in specialty occupations requiring at least a bachelor's degree or equivalent.
metaTitle: H-1B Visa Attorneys | Watson Immigration Law
metaDescription: Expert H-1B visa counsel for employers and employees. Watson Immigration Law has helped hundreds of professionals secure H-1B status. Featured in NYT, Forbes, Bloomberg.
heroHeadline: H-1B Visa Attorneys for Specialty Workers & Employers
heroSubheadline: From lottery registration to status maintenance — we handle every stage of the H-1B process.

eligibility.employer: U.S. employer must sponsor and file the petition
eligibility.degree: Bachelor's degree or higher in a specialty field (or equivalent)
eligibility.occupation: Job must qualify as a 'specialty occupation' under USCIS standards
eligibility.salary: Employer must pay the prevailing wage for the occupation and location
eligibility.cap: Subject to annual H-1B cap (65,000 regular + 20,000 advanced degree exemption)

duration: Initial 3-year period, extendable to 6 years (longer with green card sponsorship)

faqs.question: What is the H-1B visa lottery and how does it work?
faqs.answer: The H-1B lottery (officially called 'H-1B registration') is a random selection process USCIS uses when it receives more registrations than available visa numbers. Employers first register electronically (fee: $215/registration) during a March window. USCIS then randomly selects registrations to fill the cap — 65,000 for the regular cap and 20,000 for the advanced degree exemption. Selected employers are then invited to file full H-1B petitions.

faqs.question: Can I change employers on an H-1B visa?
faqs.answer: Yes. H-1B portability allows you to change employers as long as your new employer files an H-1B transfer petition before your current status expires. You can start working for the new employer as soon as the petition is filed — you don't need to wait for USCIS approval. This is one of the significant advantages of H-1B status.

faqs.question: What happens to my H-1B if I'm laid off?
faqs.answer: If you lose your H-1B job, you have a 60-day grace period to find new sponsoring employment, change to a different visa status, or depart the U.S. This grace period applies once per authorized validity period. We strongly recommend consulting with an immigration attorney immediately after a layoff to evaluate all options.

faqs.question: Can my spouse and children accompany me on an H-1B visa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your spouse and unmarried children under 21 may apply for H-4 dependent visas. H-4 visa holders may attend school and in certain circumstances — if you have an approved I-140 immigrant petition — your H-4 spouse may be eligible for an H-4 EAD (Employment Authorization Document) to work in the U.S.

faqs.question: How does the H-1B visa lead to a green card?
faqs.answer: The H-1B is a nonimmigrant visa, but it is 'dual intent,' meaning you can simultaneously pursue a green card. The typical path is: your employer sponsors you for PERM labor certification, then files an I-140 petition. Once your priority date is current, you file for adjustment of status (I-485). The H-1B can be extended beyond the 6-year cap while your green card is pending.
